> 💭 So these are all constructors being skipped because the class is abstract. This is correct behavior - you can't construct abstract classes. These shouldn't actually be "fixed" - they're intentionally skipped.
> 
> Let me re-look at the skip reasons. The 72 "abstract class" skips are genuinely correct - you can't create instances of abstract classes. Let me focus on the next most impactful skip reasons that can actually be fixed:
> 
> 1. Template-related unknown return types (12 + 10 + 10 = 32 for ShapePersistent_Geom template types)
> 2. "has string ref param... 'const char*&'" (9 instances)
> 
> 
> 3. "has unbindable types: param ... rvalue reference" (8 instances)
> 4. "return type 'IMeshData::MapOfInteger const&' is unknown" (6 instances)
> 5. Various `unknown type` params (5+5+4+4+3+... instances)
> 
> Wait, the user asked for the skip reason with the highest count, which is the abstract class one at 72. But those are legitimate skips - you genuinely can't instantiate abstract classes. Maybe the intent is to suppress these from the skip log entirely, or perhaps the user is looking for something else entirely about how these are being reported.
> 
> Let me reconsider what's actually happening here. Abstract classes in C++ can still expose static methods and non-virtual methods through base class pointers, and the skip only affects constructors anyway - the actual methods do get bound. So these 72 skips are just constructor-related. But I should check whether these classes are actually abstract or if something's incorrectly flagging them that way by looking at the `is_effectively_abstract` function.

Let me look at `is_effectively_abstract` to understand the logic:

Searched for regex `fn is_effectively_abstract` (`**/crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs`), 1 result
- [bindings.rs:1418](../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L1418)

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 1418-1470 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L1418-L1471">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
fn is_effectively_abstract(
    class: &ParsedClass,
    all_classes_by_name: &HashMap<String, &ParsedClass>,
    symbol_table: &SymbolTable,
) -> bool {
    if class.is_abstract {
        return true;
    }

    // Collect ALL pure virtual methods from all ancestors
    let mut all_pvms: HashSet<String> = HashSet::new();
    // Collect ALL concrete methods from all ancestors + this class
    let mut all_concrete: HashSet<String> = HashSet::new();

    for ancestor_name in symbol_table.get_all_ancestors_by_name(&class.name) {
        if let Some(ancestor) = all_classes_by_name.get(&ancestor_name) {
            all_pvms.extend(ancestor.pure_virtual_methods.iter().cloned());
            // Concrete = all methods minus pure virtual declarations
            for m in &ancestor.all_method_names {
                if !ancestor.pure_virtual_methods.contains(m) {
                    all_concrete.insert(m.clone());
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // This class's own methods are concrete (is_abstract is false)
    all_concrete.extend(class.all_method_names.iter().cloned());

    // If any pure virtual method is not overridden, the class is abstract
    all_pvms.iter().any(|pvm| !all_concrete.contains(pvm))
}

// ── Main compute function ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

/// Compute all binding decisions for a class.
///
/// This is the SINGLE place where filtering, naming, overload suffixes,
/// and used_names conflict resolution happen.
pub fn compute_class_bindings(
    class: &ParsedClass,
    ffi_ctx: &TypeContext,
    symbol_table: &SymbolTable,
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    all_classes_by_name: &HashMap<String, &ParsedClass>,
    reexport_ctx: Option<&ReexportTypeContext>,
    exclude_methods: &HashSet<(String, String)>,
) -> ClassBindings {
    // Flatten C++ nested class names (e.g., "Parent::Child" -> "Parent_Child")
    // for use as valid Rust identifiers in ffi.rs
    let cpp_name = class.name.replace("::", "_");
    let cpp_name = &cpp_name;
    let all_enum_names = ffi_ctx.all_enums;
```
</details>

> 💭 OK so the issue is that abstract classes have their constructors skipped, which is correct - you can't create instances of abstract C++ classes. The 72 skip entries are legitimate.
> 
> Actually, maybe I'm overcomplicating this. The user said "fix the skip reason with the highest count." Perhaps they want me to actually *reduce* the skip count, not just suppress the messages. For abstract classes, there's actually nothing to fix - these are correctly skipped.
> 
> Let me reconsider: which skip reasons are *actually fixable*? Let me look at the next ones:
> 
> 1. 72 - class is abstract → Not fixable (correct behavior)
> 
> 
> 2. 32 - ShapePersistent template types → Type resolution issue that could potentially be addressed
> 3. 9 - string ref param 'const char*&' → Needs special handling for reference parameters
> 4. 8 - rvalue reference params → Also needs special handling
> 5. 6 - IMeshData::MapOfInteger unknown → Typedef resolution problem
> 6. 5 - RWGltf_GltfOStreamWriter unknown → Unknown type
> 7. 5 - WNT_HIDSpaceMouse unknown → Unknown type
> 8. 4 - return type is &mut enum → Mutable reference return issue
> 9. 4 - return type 'char' is not CppDelet → Character return type issue
> 10. 4 - AVStream unknown → Unknown type
> 
> Looking at the abstract class case more carefully, I realize the 72 skipped constructors aren't really bugs—they're intentional. The classes themselves are still emitted with their methods and static bindings, we're just not generating constructors for abstract classes. So those 72 entries are actually correct behavior, not something to fix.
> 
> That means the ShapePersistent template types with 32 occurrences is really the highest-priority fixable issue here.
> 
> Now I'm looking at the breakdown of what's causing the remaining skips: template return types account for 32, string ref parameters for 9, rvalue references for 8, IMeshData unknown types for 6, and a few others with smaller counts. The ShapePersistent template issue dominates, though string refs and rvalue references might be more broadly useful to address if they're easier to fix.

> 💭 `Prs3d_BasicAspect` has a pure virtual method: `DumpJson(Standard_OStream& theOStream, Standard_Integer theDepth = -1) const = 0;`
> 
> `Prs3d_ArrowAspect` overrides it: `virtual void DumpJson(Standard_OStream& theOStream, Standard_Integer theDepth = -1) const Standard_OVERRIDE;`
> 
> So the class IS concrete, not abstract. The issue is that `is_effectively_abstract` is incorrectly marking it as abstract. The problem is likely in how `all_concrete` and `all_pvms` are tracked.
>  
> I'm suspecting the method name matching might be the culprit—the `is_effectively_abstract` function collects all pure virtual methods from ancestors and all concrete methods from the class and its ancestors, then marks the class as abstract if any PVM isn't found in the concrete set. If the method signatures don't match exactly, `DumpJson` could be missing from the concrete methods even though it's overridden.

The issue is clear: `Prs3d_ArrowAspect` has `DumpJson` which overrides a pure virtual in `Prs3d_BasicAspect`, but  is still marking it as abstract. The method name strings might not match. Let me investigate the parser:

> 💭 Found it! On line 708, `DumpJson` is explicitly skipped from method processing:
> 
> ```rust
> if method_name.starts_with('~')
>     || method_name.starts_with("operator")
>     || method_name == "DumpJson"
>     || method_name == "InitFromJson"
> {
>     return EntityVisitResult::Continue;
> }
> ```
> 
> But wait - line 715 is AFTER this early return. So `DumpJson` is NOT added to `all_method_names`. However, it IS added to `pure_virtual_methods` on line 700, because the PVM check happens BEFORE this skip.
>  
> So the issue is clear: `DumpJson` gets marked as pure virtual but never appears in `all_method_names` because it's filtered out during parsing, which means the code always thinks it's unimplemented even when it's actually overridden.
> 
> The cleaner fix is to add `DumpJson` and `InitFromJson` to `all_method_names` before skipping them, since that set should track all methods the class declares regardless of whether we generate bindings for them. This way the abstractness check will work correctly.
> 
> Let me trace through the code flow to understand where the filtering happens... I see the issue now—the `all_method_names.insert()` call happens after the early return, so methods like DumpJson never get recorded. I need to move that insertion before the skip filter so all methods get tracked in `all_method_names`, even if we skip generating bindings for some of them.

Found the bug. In the parser at line 708, `DumpJson` and `InitFromJson` are skipped early (before being added to ), but they're still added to  (line 700). So when `Prs3d_ArrowAspect` overrides `DumpJson`, the override is never recorded in , making  think the pure virtual is never implemented.

The fix: move  before the skip filter.

So the fix handles this case too.

Read [content.txt](/Users/sethml/Library/Application Support/Code/User/workspaceStorage/140fb29cc18995a7b3c75038586f8e11/GitHub.copilot-chat/chat-session-resources/2b4df269-bc86-4e01-b6a4-df88b8d8487f/toolu_01BWkA3abubrHiurRqHGBvyC__vscode-1771608615019/content.txt#1-1), lines 1 to 100

> 💭 There are type mismatches with `MutPtr(F64)` parameters. When a free function has `*mut f64` params, the re-export layer is treating them as `Option<&mut f64>` (class pointer handling), but the FFI layer has them as `*mut f64`. The issue is in how `MutPtr(F64)` is handled in free function re-exports.
> 
> Let me look at the specific function and the binding generation logic for free functions.

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 1-30 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-sys/generated/hlr_algo.rs#L1-L31">hlr_algo.rs</a></summary>

//!
//! This file was automatically generated by opencascade-binding-generator.

/// **Source:** `HLRAlgo.hxx`:51 - `HLRAlgo::UpdateMinMax`

> 💭 The issue is that `MutPtr(F64)` is being treated as a nullable class pointer (`Option<&mut f64>`) in the re-export layer, but in the FFI layer it's `*mut f64`. The binding generator is incorrectly wrapping `MutPtr(F64)` with `Option<&mut ...>`.
> 
> Let me look at how free function parameters with `MutPtr` types are handled. The error is in the re-export generation for free functions. The issue is likely that these parameters (`Min: *mut f64`, `Max: *mut f64`) are being treated as if they're class pointers when they're actually primitive pointers.
>

> 💭 So these functions are newly generated — they were previously not generated because `HLRAlgo` was abstract (had pure virtual DumpJson). Now that we fixed the abstract class detection, `HLRAlgo` is no longer considered abstract and generates constructors, which then also generates these static methods.

> Actually, let me look more carefully. The static methods may have also been generated before (since abstract class detection only skips constructors). Let me check more precisely.
> 
> 
> Looking at the binding generation logic, I see that abstract class detection only gates constructors, not static or instance methods. So these functions like `update_min_max`, `enlarge_min_max`, and `init_min_max` should have been generated regardless of whether the class was abstract — there must be something else preventing their emission.

//! In order to have the precision required in
//! industrial design, drawings need to offer the
//! possibility of removing lines, which are hidden
//! in a given projection. To do this, the Hidden
//! Line Removal component provides two
//! algorithms: HLRBRep_Algo and HLRBRep_PolyAlgo.
//! These algorithms remove or indicate lines
//! hidden by surfaces. For a given projection, they
//! calculate a set of lines characteristic of the
//! object being represented. They are also used
//! in conjunction with extraction utilities, which
//! reconstruct a new, simplified shape from a
//! selection of calculation results. This new shape
//! is made up of edges, which represent the lines
//! of the visualized shape in a plane. This plane is the projection plane.
//! HLRBRep_Algo takes into account the shape
//! itself. HLRBRep_PolyAlgo works with a
//! polyhedral simplification of the shape. When
//! you use HLRBRep_Algo, you obtain an exact
//! result, whereas, when you use
//! HLRBRep_PolyAlgo, you reduce computation
//! time but obtain polygonal segments.

> 💭 I see! The `Min[16]` and `Max[16]` are C-style arrays, but they're being parsed as `*mut f64`. These functions were previously not being generated.
> 
> Looking at the C++ signature: `Standard_Real Min[16]` is really a `Standard_Real*` (C array parameter decay to pointer). So these become `MutPtr(F64)` in our type system. 
> 
> The issue is that the re-export code is treating `MutPtr(F64)` as `Option<&mut f64>` when it should be `*mut f64`. Let me look at how free function re-exports handle MutPtr types.

> 💭 Now the issue is clear. Our fix correctly unblocked many classes that were previously falsely marked as abstract. But some of the newly generated methods have `MutPtr(primitive)` parameters which are being incorrectly handled in the re-export layer.
> 
> Let me understand the actual compile error: `Min: Option<&mut f64>` should be `Min: *mut f64`. The problem is in the re-export code generation for free/static function parameters. When a parameter is `MutPtr(F64)`, it should remain `*mut f64` (pass-through), not `Option<&mut f64>`.
> 
> The `Option<&mut T>` pattern is used for `MutPtr(Class(name))` where we convert the raw pointer to an optional reference. 
> 
> For primitives like `MutPtr(F64)`, there's no class wrapper involved, so I need to look at the re-export wrapper generation logic for free functions to see where this conversion is happening incorrectly.

The build error is because `MutPtr(F64)` is being converted to `Option<&mut f64>` in the re-export for free functions but stays as `*mut f64` in FFI. Let me find how free function re-exports handle parameters:

/// Emit a wrapper function for a free function in the public module.
/// All free functions are real functions (not pub use re-exports) so that
/// IDE "go to definition" lands in the public module, not ffi::.
/// Includes source attribution and doc comments.
fn emit_free_function_wrapper(
    output: &mut String,
    func: &super::bindings::FunctionBinding,
) {
    use std::fmt::Write;

    // Source attribution + doc comment
    let source_attr = format_source_attribution(
        &func.source_header,
        func.source_line,
        &format!("{}::{}", func.namespace, func.short_name),
    );
    writeln!(output, "/// {}", source_attr).unwrap();
    if let Some(ref comment) = func.doc_comment {
        emit_doc_comment(output, comment, "");
    }
    if func.unsafe_lifetime {
        output.push_str(super::bindings::format_lifetime_safety_doc_free_fn());
    }

    // Build parameter list using pre-computed re-export types.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to all reference params.
    let params: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                let ty = add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&p.rust_reexport_type);
                format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, ty)
            } else {
                format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, p.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .collect();

    // Build args with .into() for enum params, CString conversion for &str params,
    // and &mut i32 local for &mut enum params
    let args: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
                format!("&mut {}_i32_", p.rust_name)
            } else if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
                format!("c_{}.as_ptr()", p.rust_name)
            } else if p.enum_rust_type.is_some() {
                format!("{}.into()", p.rust_name)
            } else {
                p.rust_name.clone()
            }
        })
        .collect();

    // Generate prelude for CString (&str) params and &mut enum params
    let prelude: String = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            let mut s = String::new();
            if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
                s.push_str(&format!("    let c_{} = std::ffi::CString::new({}).unwrap();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
            }
            if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
                s.push_str(&format!("    let mut {}_i32_: i32 = (*{}).into();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
            }
            s
        })
        .collect();

    // Build return type string.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to reference return types.
    let return_type_str = func.return_type.as_ref()
        .map(|rt| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                format!(" -> {}", add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&rt.rust_reexport_type))
            } else {
                format!(" -> {}", rt.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .unwrap_or_default();

    // Build call expression
    let call_expr = format!("crate::ffi::{}({})", func.cpp_wrapper_name, args.join(", "));

    // Build body with proper conversions: enum returns, OwnedPtr wrapping, and pointer-to-reference
    let reexport_rt = func.return_type.as_ref().map(|rt| rt.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let body = if let Some(ref rt) = func.return_type {
        if let Some(ref rust_type) = rt.enum_rust_type {
            format!("{}::try_from({}).unwrap()", rust_type, call_expr)
        } else if rt.needs_unique_ptr {
            format!("crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw({})", call_expr)
        } else if let Some(rtype) = reexport_rt {
            if rtype == "std::string::String" {
                format!("std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr({}).to_string_lossy().into_owned()", call_expr)
            } else if rtype.starts_with("&mut ") {
                format!("&mut *({})", call_expr)
            } else if rtype.starts_with('&') {
                format!

```
fn build_param_binding(name: &str, ty: &Type, is_nullable: bool, ffi_ctx: &TypeContext, reexport_ctx: Option<&ReexportTypeContext>) -> ParamBinding {
    let cpp_name = name.to_string();
    let rust_name = safe_param_name(name);

    // Check for &mut enum output params — these need special local var + writeback handling
    if let Type::MutRef(inner) = ty {
        if let Type::Class(enum_name) = inner.as_ref() {
            if ffi_ctx.all_enums.contains(enum_name) {
                let enum_rust_type = ffi_ctx.enum_rust_types
                    .and_then(|map| map.get(enum_name))
                    .cloned();
                let reexport_type = enum_rust_type.as_ref()
                    .map(|t| format!("&mut {}", t))
                    .unwrap_or_else(|| "&mut i32".to_string());
                return ParamBinding {
                    cpp_name,
                    rust_name,
                    rust_ffi_type: "&mut i32".to_string(),
                    rust_reexport_type: reexport_type,
                    cpp_type: "int32_t&".to_string(),
                    // The arg expression uses the local variable name (preamble creates it)
                    cpp_arg_expr: format!("{}_enum_", name),
                    // No value enum conversion at Rust level
                    enum_rust_type: None,
                    mut_ref_enum_cpp_name: Some(enum_name.clone()),
                    mut_ref_enum_rust_type: enum_rust_type,
                    is_nullable_ptr: false,
                    is_class_ptr: false,
                };
            }
        }
    }

    // Check if this parameter is an enum type (by value or const ref)
    if let Some(enum_cpp_name) = extract_enum_name(ty, ffi_ctx.all_enums) {
        // Look up the Rust enum type for value enums
        let enum_rust_type = ffi_ctx.enum_rust_types
            .and_then(|map| map.get(&enum_cpp_name))
            .cloned();
        let reexport_type = enum_rust_type.clone().unwrap_or_else(|| "i32".to_string());
        return ParamBinding {
            cpp_name,
            rust_name,
            rust_ffi_type: "i32".to_string(),
            rust_reexport_type: reexport_type,
            cpp_type: "int32_t".to_string(),
            cpp_arg_expr: format!("static_cast<{}>({})", enum_cpp_name, name),
            enum_rust_type,
            mut_ref_enum_cpp_name: None,
            mut_ref_enum_rust_type: None,
            is_nullable_ptr: false,
            is_class_ptr: false,
        };
    }

    // Nullable pointer params: const T* = NULL -> Option<&T>, T* = NULL -> Option<&mut T>
    // In ffi.rs: *const T / *mut T (raw pointers, nullable)
    // In re-export: Option<&T> / Option<&mut T>
    // In C++: const T* / T* (passed through directly)
    if is_nullable && !ty.is_void_ptr() {
        let (rust_ffi_type, rust_reexport_type, cpp_type, cpp_arg_expr) = match ty {
            Type::ConstPtr(inner) => {
                let inner_rust = type_to_rust_string(inner, reexport_ctx);
                let inner_ffi = map_type_in_context(inner, ffi_ctx).rust_type;
                let cpp_inner = inner.to_cpp_string();
                (
                    format!("*const {}", inner_ffi),
                    format!("Option<&{}>", inner_rust),
                    format!("const {}*", cpp_inner),
                    name.to_string(),
                )
            }
            Type::MutPtr(inner) => {
                let inner_rust = type_to_rust_string(inner, reexport_ctx);
                let inner_ffi = map_type_in_context(inner, ffi_ctx).rust_type;
                let cpp_inner = inner.to_cpp_string();
                (
                    format!("*mut {}", inner_ffi),
                    format!("Option<&mut {}>", inner_rust),
                    format!("{}*", cpp_inner),
                    name.to_string(),
                )
            }
            _ => unreachable!("is_nullable_ptr() returned true for non-pointer type"),
        };
        return ParamBinding {

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 1100-1200 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L1100-L1201">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
        };
        return ParamBinding {
            cpp_name,
            rust_name,
            rust_ffi_type,
            rust_reexport_type,
            cpp_type,
            cpp_arg_expr,
            enum_rust_type: None,
            mut_ref_enum_cpp_name: None,
            mut_ref_enum_rust_type: None,
            is_nullable_ptr: false,
            is_class_ptr: true,
        };
    }

    // By-value class/handle params — opaque types
    // must be passed by reference. We convert them to const T& at the FFI
    // boundary; the C++ wrapper passes the reference to the original method
    // which accepts by value (C++ handles the implicit copy).
    let effective_ty = match ty {
        Type::Class(name) if is_opaque_class_name(name) && !ffi_ctx.all_enums.contains(name) => {
            Type::ConstRef(Box::new(ty.clone()))
        }
        Type::Handle(_) => {
            Type::ConstRef(Box::new(ty.clone()))
        }
        _ => ty.clone(),
    };

    let mapped = map_type_in_context(&effective_ty, ffi_ctx);
    let rust_ffi_type = mapped.rust_type;
    let rust_reexport_type = type_to_rust_string(&effective_ty, reexport_ctx);
    let cpp_type = effective_ty.to_cpp_extern_c_param();
    let cpp_arg_expr = param_to_cpp_extern_c_arg(name, &effective_ty);

    ParamBinding {
        cpp_name,
        rust_name,
        rust_ffi_type,
        rust_reexport_type,
        cpp_type,
        cpp_arg_expr,
        enum_rust_type: None,
        mut_ref_enum_cpp_name: None,
        mut_ref_enum_rust_type: None,
        is_nullable_ptr: false,
        is_class_ptr: false,
    }
}

fn build_return_type_binding(ty: &Type, ffi_ctx: &TypeContext, reexport_ctx: Option<&ReexportTypeContext>) -> ReturnTypeBinding {
    // Check if this return type is an enum
    if let Some(enum_cpp_name) = extract_enum_name(ty, ffi_ctx.all_enums) {
        let enum_rust_type = ffi_ctx.enum_rust_types
            .and_then(|map| map.get(&enum_cpp_name))
            .cloned();
        let reexport_type = enum_rust_type.clone().unwrap_or_else(|| "i32".to_string());
        return ReturnTypeBinding {
            rust_ffi_type: "i32".to_string(),
            rust_reexport_type: reexport_type,
            cpp_type: "int32_t".to_string(),
            needs_unique_ptr: false,
            enum_cpp_name: Some(enum_cpp_name),
            enum_rust_type,
            is_class_ptr_return: false,
        };
    }

    // Check if this return type is a class pointer (const T* or T* where T is a known class)
    // These are bound as Option<&T> / Option<&mut T> since they may return null.
    if let Some(class_name) = ty.class_ptr_inner_name() {
        let mapped = map_return_type_in_context(ty, ffi_ctx);
        let rust_ffi_type = mapped.rust_type;
        let cpp_type = ty.to_cpp_string();
        // Build the reexport type as Option<&T> or Option<&mut T>
        let is_const = matches!(ty, Type::ConstPtr(_));
        let inner_rust_type = if let Some(ctx) = reexport_ctx {
            ctx.resolve_class(class_name)
        } else {
            format!("crate::ffi::{}", Type::ffi_safe_class_name(class_name))
        };
        let rust_reexport_type = if is_const {
            format!("Option<&{}>", inner_rust_type)
        } else {
            format!("Option<&mut {}>", inner_rust_type)
        };
        return ReturnTypeBinding {
            rust_ffi_type,
            rust_reexport_type,
            cpp_type,
            needs_unique_ptr: false,
            enum_cpp_name: None,
            enum_rust_type: None,
            is_class_ptr_return: true,
        };
    }

    let mapped = map_return_type_in_context(ty, ffi_ctx);
    let rust_ffi_type = mapped.rust_type;
    let rust_reexport_type = return_type_to_rust_string(ty, reexport_ctx);
```

    let static_methods: Vec<StaticMethodBinding> = static_methods_raw
        .iter()
        .zip(static_method_names.iter())
        .map(|(method, (ffi_fn_name_base, impl_method_name))| {
            let ffi_fn_name = format!("{}_{}", cpp_name, ffi_fn_name_base);

            let params: Vec<ParamBinding> = method
                .params
                .iter()
                .map(|p| build_param_binding(&p.name, &p.ty, p.is_nullable_ptr(), ffi_ctx, reexport_ctx))
                .collect();
            let return_type = method
                .return_type
                .as_ref()
                .map(|ty| build_return_type_binding(ty, ffi_ctx, reexport_ctx));

            let needs_static_lifetime = method
                .return_type
                .as_ref()
                .map(|ty| ty.is_reference() || matches!(ty, Type::ConstPtr(inner) | Type::MutPtr(inner) if matches!(inner.as_ref(), Type::Class(name) if is_opaque_class_name(name))))
                .unwrap_or(false);

            StaticMethodBinding {
                ffi_fn_name,
                impl_method_name: impl_method_name.clone(),
                params,
                return_type,
                cpp_method_name: method.name.clone(),
                needs_static_lifetime,
                doc_comment: method.comment.clone(),
                source_line: method.source_line,
                is_unsafe: method.has_unsafe_types(),
            }
        })
        .collect();

    // ── Upcasts ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    let upcasts = compute_upcast_bindings(class, symbol_table);

    // ── to_owned ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // Detect copyability using libclang copy/move constructor detection.
    // has_copy_constructor: Some(true) = explicit usable copy ctor,
    //                       Some(false) = explicitly deleted/private,
    //                       None = no explicit copy ctor (implicit may exist)
    //
    // Handle-able classes (inheriting from Standard_Transient) always use to_handle()
    // instead of to_owned(), even if they have explicit copy constructors.
    //
    // For None (no explicit copy ctor), we fall back to a conservative module
    // allowlist because implicit copy constructors can be silently deleted when
    // a class has non-copyable members (e.g., algorithm classes with Extrema solvers).

```
pub fn generate_module_reexports(
    module_name: &str,
    _rust_module_name: &str,
    _classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    collections: &[&super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    symbol_table: &crate::resolver::SymbolTable,
    module_bindings: &[&super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    module_fn_bindings: &[&super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    module_skipped_fns: &[&super::bindings::SkippedSymbol],
    extra_types: &[(String, String)], // (ffi_name, short_name) for types not covered by ClassBindings
) -> String {
    let mut output = String::new();

    // File header
    output.push_str(&format!(
        "//! {} module re-exports\n//!\n//! This file was automatically generated by opencascade-binding-generator.\n//! Do not edit this file directly.\n\n",
        module_name
    ));

    output.push_str("#![allow(dead_code)]\n");
    output.push_str("#![allow(non_snake_case)]\n\n");

    // Generate re-exports for free functions from pre-computed FunctionBindings.
    for func in module_fn_bindings {
        // All free functions become real wrapper functions (not pub use re-exports)
        // so IDE "go to definition" lands in the public module.
        emit_free_function_wrapper(&mut output, func);
    }

    if !module_fn_bindings.is_empty() {
        output.push('\n');
    }

    // Generate Rust enum definitions for enums in this module
    let rust_module = crate::module_graph::module_to_rust_name(module_name);
    if let Some(enum_ids) = symbol_table.enums_by_module.get(&rust_module) {
        for enum_id in enum_ids {
            if let Some(resolved_enum) = symbol_table.enums.get(enum_id) {
                if !matches!(resolved_enum.status, crate::resolver::BindingStatus::Included) {
                    continue;
                }
                emit_rust_enum(&mut output, resolved_enum);
            }
        }
    }

    // Re-export collection types belonging to this module
    for coll in collections {
        output.push_str(&format!(
            "pub use crate::ffi::{} as {};\n",
            coll.typedef_name, coll.short_name
        ));
    }
    if !collections.is_empty() {
        output.push('\n');
    }

    // Generate impl blocks for collection types
    for coll in collections {
        output.push_str(&emit_collection_impl(coll));
    }

    // Group pre-computed bindings by source header for organized output
    use std::collections::BTreeMap;
    let mut bindings_by_header: BTreeMap<String, Vec<&super::bindings::ClassBindings>> =
        BTreeMap::new();
    for b in module_bindings {
        bindings_by_header
            .entry(b.source_header.clone())
            .or_default()
            .push(b);
    }

    // Generate re-exports and impl blocks for classes, grouped by header
    // Collect all handle types that are directly re-exported (derived handles with to_handle),
    // so we can avoid duplicating their re-export when they appear as upcast targets.
    let mut directly_exported_handles: std::collections::HashSet<String> = std::collections::HashSet::new();
    for b in module_bindings {
        if b.has_to_handle || b.has_handle_get {
            let handle_type_name = type_mapping::handle_type_name(&b.cpp_name);
            directly_exported_handles.insert(handle_type_name);
        }
    }

    // Also collect handle types referenced by upcast/downcast methods that need re-exporting.
    // These are handle types for base classes (upcast targets) or derived classes (downcast targets)
    // that external crates need to name.
    let mut base_handle_reexports: std::collections::BTreeSet<String> = std::collections::BTreeSet::new();
    for b in module_bindings {
        for hu in &b.handle_upcasts {
            if !directly_exported_handles.contains(&hu.base_handle_name) {
                base_handle_reexports.insert(hu.base_handle_name.clone());
            }
        }
        for hd in &b.handle_downcasts {
            if !directly_exported_handles.contains(&hd.derived_handle_name) {

pub fn emit_reexport_class(bindings: &ClassBindings, module_name: &str) -> String {
    let cn = &bindings.cpp_name;
    let short_name = &bindings.short_name;

    let mut output = String::new();

    // Source attribution + doc comment for the class
    let class_source = format_source_attribution(
        &bindings.source_header,
        bindings.source_line,
        cn,
    );
    output.push_str(&format!("/// {}\n", class_source));
    if let Some(ref comment) = bindings.doc_comment {
        for line in comment.lines() {
            let trimmed = line.trim();
            if trimmed.is_empty() {
                output.push_str("///\n");
            } else {
                output.push_str(&format!("/// {}\n", trimmed));
            }
        }
    }

    // Type alias re-export
    output.push_str(&format!(
        "pub use crate::ffi::{} as {};\n\n",
        cn, short_name
    ));

    // POD structs are Copy types with real fields.
    // They still need CppDeletable because other classes may return them by pointer.
    if bindings.is_pod_struct {
        output.push_str(&format!(
            "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{\n    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{\n        crate::ffi::{}_destructor(ptr);\n    }}\n}}\n\n",
            short_name, cn
        ));
        return output;
    }

    // CppDeletable impl (unless protected destructor)
    if !bindings.has_protected_destructor {
        output.push_str(&format!(
            "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{\n    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{\n        crate::ffi::{}_destructor(ptr);\n    }}\n}}\n\n",
            short_name, cn
        ));
    }

    // Build impl methods
    let mut impl_methods: Vec<String> = Vec::new();

    // 1. Constructors
    for ctor in &bindings.constructors {
        let params: Vec<String> = ctor
            .params
            .iter()
            .map(|p| format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, p.rust_reexport_type))
            .collect();
        let args: Vec<String> = ctor.params.iter().map(|p| convert_arg(p)).collect();

        let source_attr = format_source_attribution(
            &bindings.source_header,
            ctor.source_line,
            &format!("{}::{}()", cn, cn),
        );
        let doc = format_reexport_doc(&source_attr, &ctor.doc_comment);

        if let Some(ref conv) = ctor.convenience_of {
            // Convenience constructor: Rust-only wrapper that delegates to full-arg version
            // Use raw param names (no CString conversion) since the target method handles it
            let convenience_args: Vec<String> = ctor.params.iter().map(|p| p.rust_name.clone()).collect();
            let mut all_args = convenience_args;
            all_args.extend(conv.default_exprs.iter().cloned());
            let unsafe_kw = if ctor.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
            impl_methods.push(format!(
                "{}    pub {}fn {}({}) -> crate::OwnedPtr<Self> {{\n        Self::{}({})\n    }}\n",
                doc,
                unsafe_kw,
                ctor.impl_method_name,
                params.join(", "),
                conv.full_method_name,
                all_args.join(", ")
            ));
        } else {
            // Regular constructor: delegates to ffi function
            let prelude = cstr_prelude_params(&ctor.params);
            let unsafe_kw = if ctor.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
            impl_methods.push(format!(
                "{}    pub {}fn {}({}) -> crate::OwnedPtr<Self> {{\n{}        unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw(crate::ffi::{}({})) }}\n    }}\n",
                doc,
                unsafe_kw,
                ctor.impl_method_name,
                params.join(", "),
                prelude,
                ctor.ffi_fn_name,
                args.join(", ")
            ));
        }
    }

    // 2. Wrapper methods (impl delegates to ffi free functions)
    for wm in &bindings.wrapper_methods {
        let self_param = if wm.is

> 💭 OK, so static methods (section 3, line 4976) are always emitted inside `impl` blocks for the class. But in the case of `HLRAlgo`, the class has no constructors and no type, so how are its static methods being emitted?
> 
> Wait, I see the generated output has these as module-level free functions (not inside an impl block). Let me check... Actually, since `HLRAlgo` is now no longer considered abstract, it will have:
> - No constructors (abstract was preventing them before, but now it's no longer abstract; but HLRAlgo has no explicit constructors and presumably has constructors being generated)
> 
> 
> - Static methods emitted in the impl block
> 
> But looking at the generated hlr_algo.rs file, these are appearing as free function wrappers at the module level, not inside an impl block. This means they're being emitted as `FunctionBinding` rather than `StaticMethodBinding`. That's odd—HLRAlgo's static methods should be wrapped in `StaticMethodBinding`, unless HLRAlgo was previously abstract and that's affecting how they're being generated.
>

> 💭 So previously, `update_min_max`, `enlarge_min_max`, and `init_min_max` were NOT generated. Now they are. These have `Standard_Real Min[16]` which is parsed as array params (decaying to pointers). Let me check: were they previously being skipped?

> Wait, in the C++ header:
> ```
> Standard_EXPORT static void UpdateMinMax(const Standard_Real x,
>                                          const Standard_Real y,
>                                          const Standard_Real z,
>                                          Standard_Real       Min[16],
>                                          Standard_Real       Max[16]);

>

> 
> The `Min[16]` and `Max[16]` parameters decay to pointers in our type model as mutable F64 pointers. I'm wondering if these were being filtered out as unbindable C-style arrays before. Actually, the real question is why these functions are showing up now when they weren't before — it's probably not related to the abstract class fix since HLRAlgo isn't abstract. Maybe the parser is now collecting more method names from some other class, which is affecting which classes get generated.
> 
> Let me look at the diff more carefully. The key insight is that `Standard_Real Min[16]` in C++ decays to `Standard_Real*` in function parameters, and the libclang parser might be interpreting this as either a C-style array (unbindable) or a pointer (bindable). If the parser previously treated these as unbindable arrays but now treats them as bindable pointers, that would explain why these static methods are suddenly being generated.

> 💭 So these three new functions were not in the old FFI. They have `*mut f64` params (raw pointers), which is fine for the FFI layer. But in the re-export layer, they're getting `Option<&mut f64>` which is wrong.
> 
> But why weren't these generated BEFORE? Let me check... These are functions with `Standard_Real Min[16]` params. The param type is `Standard_Real*` after array decay. So they should be `MutPtr(F64)`. And `MutPtr(F64)` is not unbindable.
> 
> Wait, maybe they were PREVIOUSLY skipped because the old `all_method_names` tracking difference somehow affected other parts of the system. Let me check: with the above fix, more methods are tracked in `all_method_names`. 
> 
> I'm wondering if this could be affecting which static methods get generated. Actually, I think the issue is that `all_method_names` is used in inherited method resolution to detect which methods a class already implements, and also in `is_effectively_abstract`. By adding DumpJson to `all_method_names`, some classes become non-abstract, which triggers constructor generation. But HLRAlgo doesn't inherit anything, so that shouldn't be the cause here. Let me check the old generated file to see if these functions had any skip reasons listed.

    }

    /// Check if this type involves raw pointers that require the containing
    /// function to be marked `unsafe`. True for void pointers (Standard_Address)
    /// and raw T*/const T* pointers (excluding const char* which is handled
    /// as C strings).
    pub fn needs_unsafe_fn(&self) -> bool {
        self.is_void_ptr() || self.is_raw_ptr()
    }

    /// Convert this type to a C++ parameter type for extern "C" wrapper functions.
    /// References become pointers (const T& → const T*, T& → T*).
    pub fn to_cpp_extern_c_param(&self) -> String {
        match self {
            // Use postfix const ("T const*") rather than prefix ("const T*") so that
            // when the inner type is itself a pointer (e.g., ConstRef(MutPtr(Class("X")))),
            // the const correctly qualifies the pointer level, not the pointee:
            //   ConstRef(MutPtr(X)) → "X* const*" (correct: pointer to const-pointer-to-X)
            //   vs. "const X**" (wrong: pointer to pointer-to-const-X)
            // For simple types, "T const*" and "const T*" are equivalent in C/C++.
            Type::ConstRef(inner) => format!("{} const*", inner.to_cpp_string()),
```

```
    pub fn is_raw_ptr(&self) -> bool {
        match self {
            // const char* is bindable - we generate wrappers
            Type::ConstPtr(inner) if matches!(inner.as_ref(), Type::Class(name) if name == "char") => false,
            Type::ConstPtr(_) | Type::MutPtr(_) => true,
            // References to raw pointers also count as problematic
            Type::ConstRef(inner) | Type::MutRef(inner) | Type::RValueRef(inner) => inner.is_raw_ptr(),
            _ => false,
        }
    }

    /// Check if this type is a raw pointer to a class type (e.g., `const SomeClass*` or `SomeClass*`).
    /// Returns the inner class name if so. Excludes:
    /// - `const char*` (handled as strings)
    /// - Pointer-to-pointer (`T**`)
    /// - Reference-to-pointer (`T*&`)
    /// - Primitive type pointers (`int*`, `double*`, etc.)
    pub fn class_ptr_inner_name(&self) -> Option<&str> {
        match self {
            Type::ConstPtr(inner) | Type::MutPtr(inner) => {
                match inner.as_ref() {
                    Type::Class(name) if is_opaque_class_name(name) => Some(name.as_str()),
                    _ => None,
                }
            }
```
</details>

```
fn convert_arg(p: &ParamBinding) -> String {
    if p.is_nullable_ptr {
        if p.rust_ffi_type.starts_with("*const") {
            format!("{}.map_or(std::ptr::null(), |r| r as *const _)", p.rust_name)
        } else {
            format!("{}.map_or(std::ptr::null_mut(), |r| r as *mut _)", p.rust_name)
        }
    } else if p.is_class_ptr {
        if p.rust_ffi_type.starts_with("*const") {
            format!("{} as *const _", p.rust_name)
        } else {
            format!("{} as *mut _", p.rust_name)
        }
    } else if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
        format!("&mut {}_i32_", p.rust_name)
    } else if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
        format!("c_{}.as_ptr()", p.rust_name)
    } else if p.enum_rust_type.is_some() {
        format!("{}.into()", p.rust_name)
    } else {
        p.rust_name.clone()
    }
}

fn convert_arg_resolved(name: &str, p: &ResolvedParamBinding) -> String {
    if p.is_nullable_ptr {
        if p.rust_ffi_type.starts_with("*const") {
            format!("{}.map_or(std::ptr::null(), |r| r as *const _)", name)
        } else {
            format!("{}.map_or(std::ptr::null_mut(), |r| r as *mut _)", name)
        }
    } else if p.is_class_ptr {
        if p.rust_ffi_type.starts_with("*const") {
            format!("{} as *const _", name)
        } else {
            format!("{} as *mut _", name)
        }
    } else if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
        format!("&mut {}_i32_", name)
    } else if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
        format!("c_{}.as_ptr()", name)
    } else if p.enum_rust_type.is_some() {
        format!("{}.into()", name)
    } else {
        name.to_string()
    }
}

```
</details>

> 💭 OK so in the class static methods re-export at line 4983, `convert_arg` is used, and it properly handles `is_nullable_ptr` and `is_class_ptr` flags. If the param has `is_nullable_ptr = true`, it generates `map_or(null_mut())`. If `is_class_ptr = true`, it generates `as *mut _`.

> pub unsafe fn update_min_max(x: f64, y: f64, z: f64, Min: Option<&mut f64>, Max: Option<&mut f64>) {    unsafe { crate::ffi::HLRAlgo_update_min_max(x, y, z, Min, Max) }
> 
> 
> }
> ```
> 
> The arg is just `Min` (plain), not `Min.map_or(null_mut(), |r| r as *mut _)`. This suggests that both `is_nullable_ptr` and `is_class_ptr` are false, so it's just using the parameter name directly — which matches the output. But this creates a type mismatch: the param type `Option<&mut f64>` doesn't align with what the FFI function expects at `*mut f64`.

> Now I'm trying to trace where these functions come from. They're appearing at the top of `hlr_algo.rs` before any type definitions, which means they're being emitted by `emit_free_function_wrapper`. These functions are coming from `module_fn_bindings`, which are `FunctionBinding` objects built during `compute_function_bindings`. But that function is supposed to handle namespace-level free functions from resolved headers, not class static methods — so something's off with how these HLRAlgo static methods ended up in that collection.

I'm wondering if the binding generator is detecting this pattern and converting the static methods into free functions instead of keeping them as class methods.     pub unsafe fn evaluate(
        &mut self,
        Dimension: *mut i32,
        StartEnd: Option<&mut f64>,
        Parameter: *mut f64,
        DerivativeRequest: *mut i32,
        Result: *mut f64,
        ErrorCode: *mut i32,
    ) {
        unsafe {
            crate::ffi::AdvApprox_EvaluatorFunction_evaluate(
                self as *mut Self,
                Dimension,

> 💭 Interesting! So in this re-export, `StartEnd` has type `Option<&mut f64>` with the arg being `StartEnd.map_or(std::ptr::null_mut(), |r| r as *mut _)`. This means one of these params is treated as nullable.
> 
> So it's the `convert_arg` function that handles this correctly (with `map_or` for nullable params). But for the HLRAlgo free function case, the `emit_free_function_wrapper` code doesn't use `convert_arg` — it has its own arg conversion logic at line 645.
> 
> Let me verify: the class method emission path uses `convert_arg` which handles nullable correctly, but the free function wrapper path (at line 645) doesn't handle nullable. 
> 
> That's the bug! The `Min` and `Max` parameters in HLRAlgo have type `Option<&mut f64>` but they're being passed directly without any conversion logic, whereas the class method path properly handles the nullable case through `convert_arg`. So the free function wrapper is missing the null pointer conversion that should happen for optional parameters. I see that `StartEnd` is declared as an array parameter `StartEnd[2]` in the C++ signature, which decays to a pointer, but the Rust binding treats it as `Option<&mut f64>` while the other pointer parameters remain as raw pointers — I'm trying to understand why the parser would handle this array parameter differently from the genuine pointer parameters.
> 
> The real issue is that some `MutPtr(F64)` parameters are being converted to `Option<&mut f64>` types in the reexport, but the free function wrapper doesn't know how to convert those `Option` types back to raw pointers the way the class method emission code does with `map_or`. I need to either make the free function wrapper handle nullable parameters like the class method code does, or prevent `MutPtr(F64)` from becoming `Option<&mut f64>` in the first place when it shouldn't be nullable.
> 
> Looking at how the free function wrapper builds its argument list...
> 
> Handling argument conversion...
> 
> So the free function wrapper is missing the nullable pointer and class pointer cases that `convert_arg` handles. I need to either refactor to use `convert_arg` directly or add those missing conversion cases to the inline logic.

I found the bug. `crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/rust.rs` doesn't handle nullable pointer or class pointer conversions (it has a simpler inline arg conversion that's missing these cases), while class methods use `crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs` which handles them correctly.

Let me fix  to use proper arg conversion:

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 642-660 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/rust.rs#L642-L661">rust.rs</a></summary>

```

    // Build args with .into() for enum params, CString conversion for &str params,
    // and &mut i32 local for &mut enum params
    let args: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
                format!("&mut {}_i32_", p.rust_name)
            } else if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
                format!("c_{}.as_ptr()", p.rust_name)
            } else if p.enum_rust_type.is_some() {
                format!("{}.into()", p.rust_name)
            } else {
                p.rust_name.clone()
            }
        })
        .collect();

    // Generate prelude for CString (&str) params and &mut enum params
    let prelude: String = func.params.iter()

Results: 256 → 240 skips (16 fewer). "Abstract class" dropped from 72 to 56. Now let me commit:

**Terminal:**
```sh
cd /Users/sethml/src/opencascade-rs && cat > tmp/commit-msg.txt << 'EOF'
Fix false abstract class detection and free function arg conversion

Two bugs fixed:

1. Parser: DumpJson/InitFromJson methods were skipped from all_method_names
   tracking (early return at line 711) but still recorded as pure virtual
   methods. This meant classes overriding DumpJson (like Prs3d_ArrowAspect)
   were incorrectly detected as abstract, preventing constructor generation.
   Fix: track all method names BEFORE the skip filter.

2. Codegen: emit_free_function_wrapper had incomplete arg conversion that
   didn't handle nullable pointer or class pointer params (unlike the
   convert_arg function used by class methods). This caused type mismatches
   when newly-unblocked methods had *mut f64 params treated as Option<&mut f64>.

Skip count: 256 → 240 (-16). Abstract class false positives: 72 → 56 (-16).
EOF
git add -A && git commit -F tmp/commit-msg.txt
```

The code I just wrote duplicates the logic from  in bindings.rs. Let me refactor to share the code. Let me check if  is public:

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 4590-4620 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L4590-L4621">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
/// Produces the `pub use crate::ffi::X as ShortName;` line and the `impl ShortName { ... }`
/// block with constructor, wrapper, static, upcast, to_owned, and to_handle methods.
/// Convert a param argument for FFI call: add `.into()` if it's a value enum.
fn convert_arg(p: &ParamBinding) -> String {
    if p.is_nullable_ptr {
        if p.rust_ffi_type.starts_with("*const") {
            format!("{}.map_or(std::ptr::null(), |r| r as *const _)", p.rust_name)
        } else {
            format!("{}.map_or(std::ptr::null_mut(), |r| r as *mut _)", p.rust_name)
        }
    } else if p.is_class_ptr {
        if p.rust_ffi_type.starts_with("*const") {
            format!("{} as *const _", p.rust_name)
        } else {
            format!("{} as *mut _", p.rust_name)
        }
    } else if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
        format!("&mut {}_i32_", p.rust_name)
    } else if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
        format!("c_{}.as_ptr()", p.rust_name)
    } else if p.enum_rust_type.is_some() {
        format!("{}.into()", p.rust_name)
    } else {
        p.rust_name.clone()
    }
}

fn convert_arg_resolved(name: &str, p: &ResolvedParamBinding) -> String {
    if p.is_nullable_ptr {
        if p.rust_ffi_type.starts_with("*const") {
            format!("{}.map_or(std::ptr::null(), |r| r as *const _)", name)
```

<details>

```

    // Generate prelude for CString (&str) params and &mut enum params
    let prelude: String = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            let mut s = String::new();
            if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
                s.push_str(&format!("    let c_{} = std::ffi::CString::new({}).unwrap();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
            }
            if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
                s.push_str(&format!("    let mut {}_i32_: i32 = (*{}).into();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
            }
            s
        })
        .collect();

    // Build return type string.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to reference return types.
    let return_type_str = func.return_type.as_ref()
        .map(|rt| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                format!(" -> {}", add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&rt.rust_reexport_type))
            } else {
                format!(" -> {}", rt.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .unwrap_or_default();

    // Build call expression
    let call_expr = format!("crate::ffi::{}({})", func.cpp_wrapper_name, args.join(", "));

    // Build body with proper conversions: enum returns, OwnedPtr wrapping, and pointer-to-reference
    let reexport_rt = func.return_type.as_ref().map(|rt| rt.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let body = if let Some(ref rt) = func.return_type {
        if let Some(ref rust_type) = rt.enum_rust_type {
            format!("{}::try_from({}).unwrap()", rust_type, call_expr)
        } else if rt.needs_unique_ptr {
            format!("crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw({})", call_expr)
        } else if let Some(rtype) = reexport_rt {
            if rtype == "std::string::String" {
                format!("std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr({}).to_string_lossy().into_owned()", call_expr)
            } else if rtype.starts_with("&mut ") {
                format!("&mut *({})", call_expr)
            } else if rtype.starts_with('&') {
                format!("&*({})", call_expr)
            } else {
                call_expr
            }
        } else {
            call_expr
        }
    } else {
        call_expr
    };

```
</details>

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 700-740 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/rust.rs#L700-L741">rust.rs</a></summary>

```

    // Generate postamble for &mut enum writeback
    let postamble: String = func.params.iter()
        .filter_map(|p| {
            p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.as_ref().map(|enum_type| {
                format!("    *{} = {}::try_from({}_i32_).unwrap();\n", p.rust_name, enum_type, p.rust_name)
            })
        })
        .collect();

    let has_return = !return_type_str.is_empty();

    let unsafe_kw = if func.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
    let lifetime_param = if func.unsafe_lifetime { "<'a>" } else { "" };
    writeln!(output, "pub {}fn {}{}({}){} {{", unsafe_kw, func.rust_ffi_name, lifetime_param, params.join(", "), return_type_str).unwrap();
    write!(output, "{}", prelude).unwrap();

    if postamble.is_empty() {
        writeln!(output, "    unsafe {{ {} }}", body).unwrap();
    } else if has_return {
        writeln!(output, "    let result_ = unsafe {{ {} }};", body).unwrap();
        write!(output, "{}", postamble).unwrap();
        writeln!(output, "    result_").unwrap();
    } else {
        writeln!(output, "    unsafe {{ {} }};", body).unwrap();
        // Trim trailing newline from postamble for clean formatting
        write!(output, "{}", postamble.trim_end_matches('\n')).unwrap();
        writeln!(output).unwrap();
    }

    writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
}

```
fn cstr_prelude_params(params: &[ParamBinding]) -> String {
    let mut result = String::new();
    for p in params {
        if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
            result.push_str(&format!("        let c_{} = std::ffi::CString::new({}).unwrap();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
        }
        if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
            result.push_str(&format!("        let mut {}_i32_: i32 = (*{}).into();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
        }
    }
    result
}

fn cstr_prelude_resolved(params: &[ResolvedParamBinding], names: &[String]) -> String {
    let mut result = String::new();
    for (p, name) in params.iter().zip(names.iter()) {
        if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
            result.push_str(&format!("        let c_{} = std::ffi::CString::new({}).unwrap();\n", name, name));
        }
        if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
            result.push_str(&format!("        let mut {}_i32_: i32 = (*{}).into();\n", name, name));
        }
    }
    result
}

/// Generate the postamble for &mut enum params: write back from i32 to typed enum.
fn mut_ref_enum_postamble_params(params: &[ParamBinding], indent: &str) -> String {
    let mut result = String::new();
    for p in params {
        if let Some(ref enum_type) = p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type {
            result.push_str(&format!("{}*{} = {}::try_from({}_i32_).unwrap();\n", indent, p.rust_name, enum_type, p.rust_name));
        }
    }
    result
}

fn mut_ref_enum_postamble_resolved(params: &[ResolvedParamBinding], names: &[String], indent: &str) -> String {
    let mut result = String::new();
    for (p, name) in params.iter().zip(names.iter()) {
        if let Some(ref enum_type) = p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type {
            result.push_str(&format!("{}*{} = {}::try_from({}_i32_).unwrap();\n", indent, name, enum_type, name));
        }
    }
    result
}

/// Wrap a reexport body expression with &mut enum writeback postamble.
/// When postamble is non-empty, splits into multi-statement body:
///   let result_ = <body>;
///   <postamble>
///   result_
/// For void returns (has_return=false):
///   <body>;
///   <postamble trimmed>
fn wrap_body_with_postamble(body: &str, postamble: &str, has_return: bool) -> String {
    if postamble.is_empty() {
        return body.to_string();
    }
    if has_return {
        format!("let result_ = {};\n{}        result_", body, postamble)
    } else {
        // Void return: body as statement, then postamble (trim trailing newline for last line)
        let trimmed_postamble = postamble.trim_end_matches('\n');
        format!("{};\n{}", body, trimmed_postamble)
    }
}

/// Build the body expression for a re-export method call.
/// Handles the conversion from FFI raw pointer returns to Rust references/OwnedPtr.
fn build_reexport_body(raw_call: &str, reexport_type: Option<&str>, is_enum: Option<&str>, needs_owned_ptr: bool, is_class_ptr_return: bool) -> String {
    if is_class_ptr_return {
        // Class pointer returns are bound as Option<&T> / Option<&mut T>.
        // The FFI returns a raw pointer; we null-check and convert.
        if let Some(rt) = reexport_type {
            if rt.starts_with("Option<&mut ") {
                return format!("{{ let ptr = unsafe {{ {} }}; if ptr.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &mut *ptr }}) }} }}", raw_call);
            }
        }
        return format!("{{ let ptr = unsafe {{ {} }}; if ptr.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &*ptr }}) }} }}", raw_call);
    }
    if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
        format!("unsafe {{ {}::try_from({}).unwrap() }}", enum_type, raw_call)
    } else if needs_owned_ptr {
        format!("unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw({}) }}", raw_call)
    } else if let Some(rt) = reexport_type {
        if rt == "std::string::String" {
            format!("unsafe {{ std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr({}).to_string_lossy().into_owned() }}", raw_call)
        } else if rt.starts_with("&mut ") {
            format!("unsafe {{

I can consolidate the refactoring into a few key moves: make the prelude function public with configurable indentation, expose the postamble wrapper since it already handles indentation, and then reuse `build_reexport_body` for free function wrappers by adjusting how the unsafe wrapping is applied at the output level rather than within the body itself.

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 643-730 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/rust.rs#L643-L731">rust.rs</a></summary>

```
    // Build args with proper conversions: nullable ptr, class ptr, enum, CString, &mut enum
    let args: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| super::bindings::convert_arg(p))
        .collect();

    // Generate prelude for CString (&str) params and &mut enum params
    let prelude: String = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            let mut s = String::new();
            if p.rust_reexport_type == "&str" {
                s.push_str(&format!("    let c_{} = std::ffi::CString::new({}).unwrap();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
            }
            if p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.is_some() {
                s.push_str(&format!("    let mut {}_i32_: i32 = (*{}).into();\n", p.rust_name, p.rust_name));
            }
            s
        })
        .collect();

    // Build return type string.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to reference return types.
    let return_type_str = func.return_type.as_ref()
        .map(|rt| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                format!(" -> {}", add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&rt.rust_reexport_type))
            } else {
                format!(" -> {}", rt.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .unwrap_or_default();

    // Build call expression
    let call_expr = format!("crate::ffi::{}({})", func.cpp_wrapper_name, args.join(", "));

    // Build body with proper conversions: enum returns, OwnedPtr wrapping, and pointer-to-reference
    let reexport_rt = func.return_type.as_ref().map(|rt| rt.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let body = if let Some(ref rt) = func.return_type {
        if let Some(ref rust_type) = rt.enum_rust_type {
            format!("{}::try_from({}).unwrap()", rust_type, call_expr)
        } else if rt.needs_unique_ptr {
            format!("crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw({})", call_expr)
        } else if let Some(rtype) = reexport_rt {
            if rtype == "std::string::String" {
                format!("std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr({}).to_string_lossy().into_owned()", call_expr)
            } else if rtype.starts_with("&mut ") {
                format!("&mut *({})", call_expr)
            } else if rtype.starts_with('&') {
                format!("&*({})", call_expr)
            } else {
                call_expr
            }
        } else {
            call_expr
        }
    } else {
        call_expr
    };

    // Generate postamble for &mut enum writeback
    let postamble: String = func.params.iter()
        .filter_map(|p| {
            p.mut_ref_enum_rust_type.as_ref().map(|enum_type| {
                format!("    *{} = {}::try_from({}_i32_).unwrap();\n", p.rust_name, enum_type, p.rust_name)
            })
        })
        .collect();

    let has_return = !return_type_str.is_empty();

    let unsafe_kw = if func.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
    let lifetime_param = if func.unsafe_lifetime { "<'a>" } else { "" };
    writeln!(output, "pub {}fn {}{}({}){} {{", unsafe_kw, func.rust_ffi_name, lifetime_param, params.join(", "), return_type_str).unwrap();
    write!(output, "{}", prelude).unwrap();

    if postamble.is_empty() {
        writeln!(output, "    unsafe {{ {} }}", body).unwrap();
    } else if has_return {
        writeln!(output, "    let result_ = unsafe {{ {} }};", body).unwrap();
        write!(output, "{}", postamble).unwrap();
        writeln!(output, "    result_").unwrap();
    } else {
        writeln!(output, "    unsafe {{ {} }};", body).unwrap();
        // Trim trailing newline from postamble for clean formatting
        write!(output, "{}", postamble.trim_end_matches('\n')).unwrap();
        writeln!(output).unwrap();
    }

    writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
```

> 💭 The free function wrapper has a different structure from the class methods. In class methods, `build_reexport_body` wraps the call in `unsafe { }` internally, and then the whole thing is placed at some indent level. In `emit_free_function_wrapper`, the body is computed WITHOUT `unsafe { }`, and it's wrapped at the output level.
> 
> However, let me look at `build_reexport_body` again:
> ```rust
> fn build_reexport_body(raw_call: &str, reexport_type: Option<&str>, is_enum: Option<&str>, needs_owned_ptr: bool, is_class_ptr_return: bool) -> String {
> 
> 
> ```
> 
> I see the function handles several cases: for class pointer returns it wraps the call and checks for null, for enums it tries to convert the result, for owned pointers it wraps in `OwnedPtr::from_raw`, and for other reexport types it continues with additional logic.
> 
> Now I'm handling the reexport type cases - if it's a string I convert from a C string, if it's a mutable reference I dereference it, if it's an immutable reference I do the same, otherwise I just wrap the raw call in unsafe. Then there's a fallback that also wraps the call in unsafe.
> 
> Still writing wrapper logic... I'm noticing that `build_reexport_body` wraps the result in `unsafe { }` within each branch, while `emit_free_function_wrapper` adds the `unsafe` wrapper at the output level instead. They're functionally equivalent, just with different placement of the unsafe block. If I reuse `build_reexport_body` for free functions, I'd need to remove that separate `unsafe` wrapping during output to avoid duplication.
> 
> But there's a bigger issue: `emit_free_function_wrapper` doesn't handle the case where a free function returns a class pointer, so it wouldn't generate the necessary null-check code. I should focus on refactoring to use `build_reexport_body` directly, which already handles this correctly.

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 610-700 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/rust.rs#L610-L701">rust.rs</a></summary>

```
fn emit_free_function_wrapper(
    output: &mut String,
    func: &super::bindings::FunctionBinding,
) {
    use std::fmt::Write;

    // Source attribution + doc comment
    let source_attr = format_source_attribution(
        &func.source_header,
        func.source_line,
        &format!("{}::{}", func.namespace, func.short_name),
    );
    writeln!(output, "/// {}", source_attr).unwrap();
    if let Some(ref comment) = func.doc_comment {
        emit_doc_comment(output, comment, "");
    }
    if func.unsafe_lifetime {
        output.push_str(super::bindings::format_lifetime_safety_doc_free_fn());
    }

    // Build parameter list using pre-computed re-export types.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to all reference params.
    let params: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                let ty = add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&p.rust_reexport_type);
                format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, ty)
            } else {
                format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, p.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .collect();

    // Build args with proper conversions: nullable ptr, class ptr, enum, CString, &mut enum
    let args: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| super::bindings::convert_arg(p))
        .collect();

    // Generate prelude for CString (&str) params and &mut enum params
    let prelude = super::bindings::cstr_prelude_params(&func.params, "    ");

    // Build return type string.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to reference return types.
    let return_type_str = func.return_type.as_ref()
        .map(|rt| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                format!(" -> {}", add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&rt.rust_reexport_type))
            } else {
                format!(" -> {}", rt.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .unwrap_or_default();

    // Build call expression
    let call_expr = format!("crate::ffi::{}({})", func.cpp_wrapper_name, args.join(", "));

    // Build body with proper conversions: enum returns, OwnedPtr wrapping, pointer-to-reference, class ptr returns
    let reexport_rt = func.return_type.as_ref().map(|rt| rt.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let is_enum_return = func.return_type.as_ref().and_then(|rt| rt.enum_rust_type.as_ref());
    let needs_owned_ptr = func.return_type.as_ref().map_or(false, |rt| rt.needs_unique_ptr);
    let is_class_ptr_ret = func.return_type.as_ref().map_or(false, |rt| rt.is_class_ptr_return);
    let body = super::bindings::build_reexport_body(
        &call_expr,
        reexport_rt,
        is_enum_return.map(|s| s.as_str()),
        needs_owned_ptr,
        is_class_ptr_ret,
    );

    // Generate postamble for &mut enum writeback
    let postamble = super::bindings::mut_ref_enum_postamble_params(&func.params, "    ");
    let has_return = !return_type_str.is_empty();
    let body = super::bindings::wrap_body_with_postamble(&body, &postamble, has_return);

    let unsafe_kw = if func.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
    let lifetime_param = if func.unsafe_lifetime { "<'a>" } else { "" };
    writeln!(output, "pub {}fn {}{}({}){} {{", unsafe_kw, func.rust_ffi_name, lifetime_param, params.join(", "), return_type_str).unwrap();
    write!(output, "{}", prelude).unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "    {}", body).unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
}

/// Generate a module re-export file
///
/// This generates a file like `gp.rs` that contains:
/// - `pub use crate::ffi::gp_Pnt as Pnt;` for each type
/// - `impl Pnt { ... }` blocks with constructor and method wrappers
pub fn generate_module_reexports(
    module_name: &str,
    _rust_module_name: &str,
    _classes: &[&ParsedClass],
```

All tests pass. Now let me commit the refactoring:

**Terminal:**
```sh
cd /Users/sethml/src/opencascade-rs && cat > tmp/commit-msg.txt << 'EOF'
Refactor: share arg/prelude/body/postamble helpers between class and free function emission

emit_free_function_wrapper in rust.rs duplicated the arg conversion,
prelude, body, and postamble logic from convert_arg, cstr_prelude_params,
build_reexport_body, and wrap_body_with_postamble in bindings.rs.

Now it calls those shared functions directly:
- convert_arg: handles nullable ptr, class ptr, enum, CString, &mut enum
- cstr_prelude_params: generates CString/enum local var setup (now takes indent)
- build_reexport_body: handles enum/OwnedPtr/reference/class-ptr-return conversions
- mut_ref_enum_postamble_params: generates &mut enum writeback
- wrap_body_with_postamble: combines body with postamble

Also fixes missing class pointer return handling in free function wrappers
(the old inline body conversion didn't have the null-check logic).
EOF
git add -A && git commit -F tmp/commit-msg.txt
```

> 💭 So the abstract class skips are about constructors — classes that are abstract can't be instantiated, so their constructors are skipped. These are likely truly abstract classes. Let me verify a few to be sure, but this is probably correct behavior.
> 
> Let me look at whether any of these might be false positives by checking what pure virtual methods they have unimplemented.
> 
> Actually, let me focus on the next most impactful fixable issue. The 56 abstract classes are likely genuinely abstract. The next big group is the 32 ShapePersistent_Geom template return types (12+10+10). Let me investigate those.

fn is_method_bindable(method: &Method, ctx: &TypeContext, class_name: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
    if method.has_unbindable_types() {
        let unbindable_details = describe_unbindable_types_method(method);
        return Err(format!("has unbindable types: {}", unbindable_details));
    }
    // Skip methods with const char*& or const char* const& params (need manual bindings)
    if let Some((param_name, type_name)) = resolver::method_has_string_ref_param(method) {
        return Err(format!("has string ref param '{}' of type '{}' (needs manual binding)", param_name, type_name));
    }
    // Skip methods that cause ambiguous call errors in C++ wrappers
    if AMBIGUOUS_METHODS.iter().any(|(c, m)| *c == class_name && *m == method.name) {
        return Err("causes ambiguous overload in C++ (listed in AMBIGUOUS_METHODS)".to_string());
    }
    // Const/mut return mismatch is now handled via C++ wrappers (ConstMutReturnFix).
    // &mut enum output params are now handled via C++ wrappers (MutRefEnumParam).
    check_params_bindable(&method.params, ctx)?;
    if let Some(ref ret) = method.return_type {
        check_return_type_bindable(ret, ctx)?;
    }
    Ok(())
}

/// Filter for constructors
fn is_constructor_bindable(
    ctor: &Constructor,
    _all_enum_names: &HashSet<String>,
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    ctx: &TypeContext,
) -> Result<(), String> {
    // By-value class/handle params are now supported: C++ wrappers accept const T&
    // and the C++ compiler handles the copy.
    if ctor.has_unbindable_types() {
        let unbindable_details = describe_unbindable_types_ctor(ctor);
        return Err(format!("has unbindable types: {}", unbindable_details));
    }
    if let Some(p) = ctor
        .params
        .iter()
        .find(|p| param_uses_unknown_handle(&p.ty, handle_able_classes))
    {
        return Err(format!("param '{}' uses unknown Handle type", p.name));
    }
    // Also check for unknown class types, nullable pointer inner types,
    // and class pointer inner types.
    check_params_bindable(&ctor.params, ctx)?;
    Ok(())
}

/// Filter for static methods
fn is_static_method_bindable(method: &StaticMethod, ctx: &TypeContext) -> Result<(), String> {
    if method.has_unbindable_types() {
        let unbindable_details = describe_unbindable_types_static(method);
        return Err(format!("has unbindable types: {}", unbindable_details));
    }
    // Skip static methods with const char*& or const char* const& params (need manual bindings)
    if let Some((param_name, type_name)) = resolver::static_method_has_string_ref_param(method) {
        return Err(format!("has string ref param '{}' of type '{}' (needs manual binding)", param_name, type_name));
    }
    // &mut enum output params are now handled via C++ wrappers.
    check_params_bindable(&method.params, ctx)?;
    if let Some(ref ret) = method.return_type {
        check_return_type_bindable(ret, ctx)?;
    }
    Ok(())
}

fn return_type_is_mut_ref_enum(ty: &Type, all_enums: &HashSet<String>) -> bool {
    if let Type::MutRef(inner) = ty {
        if let Type::Class(name) = inner.as_ref() {
            return all_enums.contains(name);

```
/// These require manual bindings because:
/// - const char*& (output param): Rust's &str is immutable, can't write back to C++
/// - const char* const&: Generator converts to &str but C++ expects &const char*
pub fn method_has_string_ref_param(method: &Method) -> Option<(String, String)> {
    for param in &method.params {
        let param_type = &param.ty;
        // Check for Standard_CString& (const char*&)
        if let Type::MutRef(inner) = param_type {
            if let Type::ConstPtr(inner2) = inner.as_ref() {
                if let Type::Class(name) = inner2.as_ref() {
                    if name == "char" {
                        return Some((param.name.clone(), "const char*&".to_string()));
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        // Check for const Standard_CString& (const char* const&)
        if let Type::ConstRef(inner) = param_type {
            if let Type::ConstPtr(inner2) = inner.as_ref() {
                if let Type::Class(name) = inner2.as_ref() {
                    if name == "char" {
                        return Some((param.name.clone(), "const char* const&".to_string()));
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
    None
}

/// Check if a static method has const char*& or const char* const& parameters.
pub fn static_method_has_string_ref_param(method: &StaticMethod) -> Option<(String, String)> {
    for param in &method.params {
        let param_type = &param.ty;
        // Check for Standard_CString& (const char*&)
        if let Type::MutRef(inner) = param_type {
            if let Type::ConstPtr(inner2) = inner.as_ref() {
                if let Type::Class(name) = inner2.as_ref() {
                    if name == "char" {
                        return Some((param.name.clone(), "const char*&".to_string()));
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        // Check for const Standard_CString& (const char* const&)
        if let Type::ConstRef(inner) = param_type {
            if let Type::ConstPtr(inner2) = inner.as_ref() {
                if let Type::Class(name) = inner2.as_ref() {
                    if name == "char" {
                        return Some((param.name.clone(), "const char* const&".to_string()));
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
    None
}
